narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much effort and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 32.50, Year 1 school decile ranking of 2nd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 3rd out of 10, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time—compared to typical students who average higher GPAs around 3.21, LSATs near 36.70, and mid-decile rankings like 5th or 6th. The model predicted a 55% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those with stronger predicted odds of passing. This prediction was shaped most strongly by your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by suggesting a better shot at passing than average. However, your Year 3 decile ranking had the next biggest pull toward the failure prediction, indicating it was lower than what typically leads to bar success. Your family's upper-middle income status also nudged the model toward predicting failure, as it differs from patterns seen in passers. Your LSAT score followed closely, contributing toward the risk of failure relative to higher-scoring peers, while your lower Year 1 decile added a smaller push in the same direction. Interestingly, your choice to go full-time helped a bit, aligning with the vast majority of students who pass. Overall, these factors combined to tip the scales against you compared to typical admitted students with stronger academic rankings and test scores. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework, or exploring part-time options or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to build a more competitive profile. Many have turned similar setbacks into success, and we're rooting for you to reapply stronger.
